reactions.name: Aldol condensation
reactions.description: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo aldol condensation under mildly basic conditions to form glycolaldehyde, the simplest sugar-related aldehyde.
reactions.conditions: Mildly basic aqueous solution, mineral surface catalysis (e.g. clay or carbonate minerals), UV irradiation

reactions.name: Formose-type oligomerization
reactions.description: Glycolaldehyde reacts with additional formaldehyde via sequential aldol condensations and retro-aldol rearrangements to yield the symmetric C4 dialdehyde succinaldehyde.
reactions.conditions: Aqueous solution, moderate temperature (60-100 °C), mineral catalysis, formose-type reaction cascade

reactions.name: Intramolecular cyclization
reactions.description: Succinaldehyde reacts with ammonia: one aldehyde forms a C=N imine bond, then the nitrogen attacks the second aldehyde intramolecularly, closing the five-membered ring with loss of water to produce delta-1-pyrroline.
reactions.conditions: Aqueous solution, ambient to moderate temperature, spontaneous at neutral to mildly acidic pH

reactions.name: Strecker addition
reactions.description: HCN undergoes nucleophilic addition across the C=N imine bond of delta-1-pyrroline, installing the cyanide group at C-2 of the pyrrolidine ring. This is the key carbon-nitrogen bond-forming step that introduces the carboxylate precursor.
reactions.conditions: Aqueous solution with dissolved HCN, mildly acidic to neutral pH, ambient temperature

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis
reactions.description: The nitrile group (-CN) of 2-cyanopyrrolidine is hydrolyzed stepwise through an amide intermediate to the free carboxylic acid, releasing ammonia and yielding racemic proline.
reactions.conditions: Aqueous solution, elevated temperature (50-100 °C) or prolonged time at ambient temperature, acidic or basic catalysis

reactions.name: Chiral amplification
reactions.description: The racemic DL-proline mixture is resolved to the biological L-enantiomer via symmetry-breaking mechanisms: circularly polarized light, enantioselective adsorption on chiral mineral surfaces (calcite, quartz), or Soai-type autocatalytic amplification. L-proline itself acts as an asymmetric organocatalyst, enabling self-amplification of enantiomeric excess.
reactions.conditions: Chiral mineral surfaces (e.g. d-calcite), circularly polarized UV light, or autocatalytic amplification via asymmetric aldol catalysis
